narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a high family income bracket, along with a full-time study plan that's common among most students, you've put together a competitive profile for law school admission. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 60.5%, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize applicants with stronger projected bar outcomes. The primary factor driving this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, which strongly signaled to the model a heightened risk of bar exam struggles compared to peers in higher deciles. Reinforcing this, your Year 1 decile of 3—lower than the typical 5.71—further pushed the prediction toward failure, suggesting early academic challenges that carried over. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the odds toward passing since it exceeds the norm and demonstrates capability at the college level. While your LSAT score of 35 is slightly below the 36.70 average, it wasn't among the top influences here. Overall, your situation stands out from typical admitted students because lower high school deciles like yours are rare among those predicted to pass the bar easily, even with strengths like your GPA and background. This model weighs these patterns heavily to forecast long-term success in law. To move forward, consider retaking classes or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps through community college or targeted prep that addresses those early weaknesses. Strengthening your LSAT could also help offset this in future applications. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by focusing on academic recovery—reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and reapply once you've built a stronger record. Your high family support and full-time commitment position you well for that turnaround.
